data.title: Publix Reports First Quarter 2026 Results and Stock Price
data.description:
LAKELAND, Fla.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--May 01, 2026--

Publix's sales for the three months ended March 28, 2026 were $16.1 billion, a 2% increase from $15.8 billion in 2025. The increase in sales for the three months ended March 28, 2026 compared to the three months ended March 29, 2025 was lower due to the impact of the Medicare maximum fair price (MFP) change effective January 1, 2026 which reduced drug prices for 10 drugs. The impact of the MFP change resulted in a decrease in sales. Comparable store sales for the three months ended March 28, 2026 remained unchanged.

Net earnings for the three months ended March 28, 2026 were $794 million, compared to $1 billion in 2025, a decrease of 21.5%. Earnings per share for the three months ended March 28, 2026 decreased to $0.25 per share, down from $0.31 per share in 2025. Excluding the impact of net unrealized losses on equity securities in 2026 and 2025, net earnings for the three months ended March 28, 2026 would have been $1.1 billion, compared to $1.2 billion in 2025, a decrease of 3%. Earnings per share for the three months ended March 28, 2026 and March 29, 2025 would have been $0.36 per share.

These amounts are based on unaudited financial statements that will be filed today with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission and made available on the company's website at corporate.publix.com/stock.

Effective May 1, 2026, Publix's stock price increased from $19.65 per share to $20.45 per share. Publix stock is not publicly traded and is made available for sale only to current Publix associates and members of its board of directors.

"Our associates are the foundation of everything we do," said Publix CEO Kevin Murphy. "They deliver exceptional service to our customers and make a meaningful difference in the communities we serve every day."

Non-GAAP Financial Measures

In addition to reporting financial results for the three months ended March 28, 2026 and March 29, 2025 in accordance with U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP), the company presents net earnings and earnings per share excluding the impact of equity securities being measured at fair value with net unrealized gains and losses from changes in the fair value recognized in earnings (fair value adjustment). These measures are not in accordance with, or an alternative to, GAAP. The company excludes the impact of the fair value adjustment since it is primarily due to temporary equity market fluctuations that do not reflect the company's operations. The company believes this information is useful in providing period-to-period comparisons of the results of operations.

Following is a reconciliation of net earnings to net earnings excluding the impact of the fair value adjustment for the three months ended March 28, 2026 and March 29, 2025:

Publix, the largest employee-owned company in the U.S. with more than 260,000 associates, currently operates 1,434 stores in Florida, Georgia, Alabama, Tennessee, South Carolina, North Carolina, Virginia and Kentucky. For 29 consecutive years, the company has been recognized by Fortune as a great place to work. In addition, Publix's dedication to superior quality and customer service is recognized among the top in the grocery business. For more information, visit the company's newsroom at corporate.publix.com/newsroom.

data.title: Ares Management Stock Slips After Earnings. Private Credit Worries Remain. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jack Denton

Ares Management stock slipped on Friday after the alternative investment manager reported first-quarter earnings that fanned worries about private credit.

The group reported net income of $142.6 million, or 46 cents a share. The stock was down 1%.

"We reported strong first quarter results..., including record first quarter fund-raising of $30 billion, up more than 45% year over year," said CEO Michael Arougheti. "We are on track for another record year of fund-raising as we continue to see broad-based investor demand across our platform."

Despite Friday's lift, the stock has been in the dumps — shares have lost a quarter of their value in the past six months.

Investors are worried about the health of the private credit industry; several funds endured what amounted to a bank run in April.

"We...continue to see strong fundamental performance across our investment portfolios despite the volatile market environment," said Arougheti.

Blue Owl Capital, another alternative investment manager, reported better-than-expected profit on Thursday, sending the stock almost sending the stock almost 10% higher. Shares were up 1.5% again on Friday.

Blue Owl, which emphasized other areas of business besides private credit, has seen its stock drop almost 40% in the last six months.

Write to Jack Denton at jack.denton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Imperial Oil Q1 Net Profit Declines; Declares Dividend; Stock Down 5.2%
data.description:
OTTAWA (dpa-AFX) - Imperial Oil Ltd. (IMO, IMO.TO) reported on Friday that first-quarter net income declined to C$940 million or C$1.94 per share from C$1.29 billion or C$2.52 per share in the prior-year quarter.

Total revenues and other income for the quarter edged down to C$12.45 billion from C$12.52 billion in the same quarter last year.

'I am confident in our ability to generate superior cash flow, underpinning our reliable and growing dividend and our industry-leading share buyback program,' said John Whelan, chairman, president and chief executive officer.

Separately, the company declared a quarterly dividend of 87 cents per share on the outstanding common shares of the company, payable on July 1, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on June 4, 2026.

In Friday's regular trading session, IMO is trading on the NYSE at $127.10, down $6.92 or 5.16 percent.

data.title: AutoNation Says War In Iran Will Weigh on Car Sales — Market Talk
data.description: AutoNation says affordability concerns are worsening due to the war in Iran. The car retailer was expecting affordability issues to weigh on consumers somewhat this year, which management was estimating would have a 5% impact on the new vehicle sales across the industry, CEO Mike Manley says. Fuel price inflation from the Middle East conflict is compounding those issues, he says, and is likely to continue to do so. Manley says he now expects the impact from affordability issues will now be greater than 5% forecast until there are improvements in the war, gas prices, interest rates or other macroeconomic factors. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: LINDE PLC 1Q 2026: Revenue $8.78B, EPS $3.98— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
LINDE PLC reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $8.78B, up from $8.11B a year earlier, and diluted EPS of $3.98, reflecting higher net income of $1.9B driven by pricing, currency translation and volume from new project start-ups.

Financial Highlights

Revenue¹$8.78B$8.11B8.2%
Net income²$1.9B$1.71B11.3%
Diluted EPS³$3.98$3.5113.4%

¹ Reported as “Sales”. ² Reported as “Net Income (Including Noncontrolling Interests)”. ³ Reported as “Diluted earnings per share”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth in the quarter was supported by pricing, favorable currency translation and volume from new project start-ups.
  • Regional performance was led by the Americas, up 10%, and APAC, up 11%; EMEA rose 7% despite declines in manufacturing volumes.
  • Operating profit increased about 12% as productivity initiatives and pricing offset cost inflation.
  • Capital expenditures rose to $1.34B to fund new plants and equipment, and the gas backlog for large projects stood at roughly $7.1B.

Original SEC Filing: LINDE PLC [ LIN ] - 10-Q - May. 01, 2026

data.title: Defense Department Notches AI Deals With Nvidia, Amazon, and 5 Others. Who Was Left Out? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

The Department of Defense reached agreements with seven of the world's leading technology companies to deploy artificial intelligence across the agency's classified networks.

Nvidia, Microsoft, Alphabet's Google, SpaceX, ChatGPT maker OpenAI, Amazon.com's cloud arm Amazon Web Services, and frontier AI start-up Reflection will all serve as AI vendors.

The stocks weren't getting much of a boost from the announcement. Amazon fell 0.9% and Google parent Alphabet was down 0.2%. Nvidia and Microsoft each rose less than 1%.

The deals are designed to help establish the U.S. military "as an AI-first fighting force," the Pentagon said in a news release Friday. It will use AI to "streamline data synthesis, elevate situational understanding, and augment warfighter decision-making in complex operational environments."

The names left out of the Pentagon's network of AI vendors may be more significant than those included. Among the Magnificent Seven's AI luminaries, Meta Platforms was the notable absentee.

Unsurprisingly, AI lab Anthropic didn't join its rival OpenAI on the list, having been locked in a dispute with the Trump administration over the use of AI-trained weapons.

Emil Michael, the Department of Defense's chief technology officer, confirmed on CNBC's "Squawk Box" Friday that Anthropic remains blacklisted after being deemed a "supply chain risk" earlier this year. The company sued the Trump administration over the label in March.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Here's what B. of A.'s Michael Hartnett thinks investors should buy and sell in May
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

The U.S. long bond needs to hold 5% to keep the 'boom loop' intact

Strength in semiconductors suggests a cyclical uptick looming in the U.S. economy if the Treasury can keep a lid on bond yields.

Semiconductors SOX, oil shares USO, Taiwan EWT, tech stocks XLK and AI plays BAI are all overbought and look precariously high relative to their 50-day and 200-day moving averages. Conversely, the most oversold trades are China tech KWEB, global defense stocks SHLD, healthcare XLV and Treasury bonds ZROZ. Any whiff of good news from the Persian Gulf and these are all set for trend reversal.

These are what Bank of America's chief equity strategist, Michael Hartnett, calls "the buys and sells of May" (with a nod to Shakespeare's sonnet), and he enumerates them in his weekly Flow Show report, published Friday. Hartnett suspects the oversold and the overbought may start to reverse respective directions if his hunch is correct that there's a cyclical upswing in the U.S. economy looming.

As the best evidence for this, Hartnett cites the Institute for Supply Management's monthly index, which he thinks will surge from 53 last time round toward 60. Surveying the cyclicals, Hartnett and his team of Anya Shelekhin, Myung-Jee Jung and Jessica Guo think China, Europe, the equal-weighted S&P 500 UK:EWSP and materials XME are better vehicles for this shift than the likes of Japan (NIY00), Korea KR:180721 and emerging markets EEM (ex China) that have already discounted it.

Strength in semiconductors may imply that the ISM will surge to 60 from present levels around 53.

The crucial determinant of market direction, however, is what Hartnett calls the Maginot Line of the 5% level on the 30-year U.S. Treasury bond BX:TMUBMUSD30Y. While the line of French defensive fortresses famously did not hold against the Germans in 1940, Hartnett thinks the long bond can hold the line at 5%.

Hartnett believes the U.S. long bond can hold its psychologically vital support level at 5%.

Hartnett notes the White House is "on maneuvers" to maintain the interest of big investors in U.S. Treasurys. Central banks and major institutions in the Middle East and Asia hold $3.8 trillion of these instruments, and Hartnett thinks keeping them sweet by supporting the dollar is part of the strategy. Moreover, the White House and the Treasury Department need to reverse "the deflation of Trump's inflation approval" rating, which has sunk to 29%, on par with Joe Biden's 28% when he was dealing with a postpandemic spike in consumer prices that was closer to an annualized 9% than 3.5%.

Were the 5% level to crack, then Hartnett warns what has been a "boom loop" of fast-rising nominal GDP (encouraged by governments' fiscal profligacy and policies supportive of financial markets) could mutate into a "doom loop" instead. A doom loop could trigger a sharp jump in yields like that which occurred during similar events in Japan in 1989, in the U.S. in 1999 and China in 2007.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Sterling Could Stay Range-Bound Vs Euro — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling could continue to trade in a narrow range against the euro after Thursday's policy decisions from the Bank of England and European Central Bank, MUFG Bank analyst Derek Halpenny says in a note. There was little in the two central banks' decisions to suggest much in the way of policy divergence, he says. It is "clear to us that without a dramatic reversal in energy prices between now and the June meetings, both the BOE and ECB will be hiking rates." The ECB and BOE both left rates unchanged Thursday as expected. The euro rises 0.1% to 0.8626 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Yields fall as Iran submits fresh US negotiation proposal
data.description:
Key points:
  • Iran's proposal raises hopes for conflict resolution, easing oil and bond market pressures
  • Fed leaves rates unchanged, but hawkish tone and dissent cloud rate cut outlook
  • Markets focus on upcoming jobs data and Treasury funding guidance for economic signals

By Karen Brettell

U.S. Treasury yields fell Friday alongside oil prices after Iran submitted a fresh proposal to Pakistani mediators for negotiations with the United States, raising hopes for a nearer-term resolution to the conflict.

The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has choked off 20% of the world's oil and gas supplies, and the U.S. Navy is blocking exports of Iranian crude oil.

Yields have risen over the past two weeks on concerns that ongoing energy disruptions will keep oil prices elevated and fuel a renewed increase in inflation, which is already running above the Federal Reserve's 2% annual target.

Developments in the Middle East have contributed to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook, overshadowing other market factors despite generally improving U.S. economic data.

“The market is less responsive to economic data in a backdrop that's been so battered by the back and forth of the geopolitical headlines,” said Jonathan Cohn, head of U.S. rates desk strategy at Nomura.

The market also weakened on Wednesday after the Federal Reserve's meeting produced a more hawkish outcome than expected.

The Fed left its benchmark overnight interest rate unchanged, as expected, but three members dissented over language suggesting the central bank could eventually resume rate cuts.

“The easing bias on the Fed is weaker, with more Fed participants pushing for symmetric language,” said Cohn. “From the market's perspective, I don't necessarily see a clear read-through given we have already had a very meaningful repricing of policy expectations. Right now markets are expecting very little over the next year.”

Fed funds futures show that markets expect the U.S. central bank to stay on hold for the near term, though the probability of a hike increased in early next year.

The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with Fed interest rate expectations, fell 2.6 basis points to 3.859%. The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 3.2 basis points to 4.358%.

The yield curve between two- and 10-year notes (US2US10=TWEB) was little changed at 50 basis points.

Jobs data will be the main economic focus next week, culminating in Friday’s payrolls report for April. Employers are expected to have added 60,000 jobs during the month, according to the median estimate of economists polled by Reuters.

The U.S. Treasury will also provide its updated funding estimates for the coming two quarters.

“I think a question that will be debated going into next week is whether or not Treasury decides to amend its forward guidance regarding no changes for at least the next several quarters,” Cohn said.

The Treasury repeated the guidance at its last update in February.

data.title: Five killed in small plane crash near Austin
data.description:
Five people were killed late Thursday when a small plane crashed near Austin, Texas, local authorities said Friday.

The National Transportation Safety Board and Federal Aviation Administration will investigate the crash of a Cessna 421C in Wimberley, Texas, around 11:25 p.m. Thursday. The site is about 40 miles from Austin.

The Hayes County Homeland Security and Emergency Management office said emergency personnel responded to the scene and added preliminary information indicates the aircraft was traveling at a high rate of speed at the time of impact.

The agency said a second aircraft traveling in the vicinity around the time of the crash landed safely in New Braunfels.

data.title: Roku Stock Looks Like a Streaming Wars Winner — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Roku underlined its status as a potential streaming wars winner by beating on first-quarter earnings and raising its guidance, sending its shares higher on Friday.

The stock rose 1.3% to $118.12 in early trading. The S&P 500 was 0.5% higher.

Roku makes streaming devices and licenses its operating system to TV makers. That means it can make money from both advertising and subscriptions to its service. Advertising revenue jumped 27% from a year ago to $616 million, and subscription revenue surged 24% from a year ago to $518 million.

Both revenue streams "offer engagement and monetization upside," Seaport Research Partners analyst David Joyce said, raising his price target to $138 from $130.

The company gets a cut of subscription fees for channels and streaming services that are bought through its platform. Deals with major streaming platforms including Apple TV and Peacock boosted subscription revenue over the first quarter.

"As the firm extends its leading connected TV platform, it becomes more difficult for any competitor to displace Roku's place in the streaming ecosystem," Morningstar analyst Matthew Dolgin said in a research note, raising his fair value estimate for the stock to $95 from $85.

The only downside is that Roku's full-year guidance looked a little conservative despite a significant earnings beat, he added.

For the first quarter, Roku reported adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (Ebitda) of $148 million, as revenue jumped 22% from a year ago to $1.25 billion. Analysts were expecting Ebitda of $131 million on revenue of $1.20 billion, according to a FactSet poll.

For the full year, Roku expects Ebitda of $675 million on revenue of $5.54 billion. Wall Street was forecasting Ebitda of $644 million on revenue of $5.51 billion ahead of the earnings report.

Roku shares were up 88% over the past year as of Friday, surging on the expectation that the shift from linear TV to streaming will be a tailwind for earnings.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Barbie Gets Stamp of Approval from USPS — Market Talk
data.description: After decades in dream houses, Barbie is moving into America's mailboxes. The dolls this summer will deliver first-class fashion, as the U.S. Postal Service is gearing up to release a set of stamps featuring the iconic toys. The set of stamps will showcase 10 of the more than 250 careers that Barbie has represented over her more than 65-year history, USPS says. The Post Office collaborated with Mattel on the designs of the stamps, which will employ a rarely used format that is one-third taller than the usual vertical commemorative stamp and further engrave Barbie into the upper echelons of American history and culture. Ethel Kessler, an art director at USPS, says the team approached the project "with a spirit of fun," using bright colors and lots of "Barbie Pink" to evoke a feeling of joy. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 69 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 63 cents. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 67 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 63 cents to 75 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 14.6% to $477.50 million from a year ago; analysts expected $474.34 million.

  • Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 59 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $48.4 million.

  • Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc shares had risen by 11.7% this quarter and gained 22.1% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 3.3% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 4 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the auto, truck & motorcycle parts peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc is $69.50, about 8.8% above its last closing price of $63.40

data.title: AEM: Record net income and robust growth pipeline position the company for sustained expansion
data.description:
Record Q1 results driven by strong operations, high gold prices, and disciplined cost control, with net income and EBITDA at all-time highs. Production and cost guidance for 2026 are reiterated, and major growth projects and exploration support a 20–30% production increase over the next decade.
